analogy: Think of "Moving Charges and Magnetism" through the chapter's own opening images: 4.1 INTRODUCTION Both Electricity and Magnetism have been known for more than 2000 years. However, it was only about 200 years ago, in 1820, that it was realised that they were intimately related. During a lecture demonstration in the summer of 1820, Danish physicist Hans... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Moving Charges and Magnetism is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 4.1 INTRODUCTION Both Electricity and Magnetism have been known for more than 2000 years.

The text explains that however, it was only about 200 years ago, in 1820, that it was realised that they were intimately related.

The text explains that during a lecture demonstration in the summer of 1820, Danish physicist Hans Christian Oersted noticed that a current in a straight wire caused a noticeable deflection in a nearby magnetic compass needle.

The text explains that he found that the alignment of the needle is tangential to an imaginary circle which has the straight wire as its centre and has its plane perpendicular to the wire.

The text explains that it is noticeable when the current is large and the needle sufficiently close to the wire so that the earth’s magnetic field may be ignored.

The text explains that reversing the direction of the current reverses the orientation of the needle [Fig.

The text explains that the deflection increases on increasing the current or bringing the needle closer to the wire.

The text explains that iron filings sprinkled around the wire arrange themselves in concentric circles with the wire as the centre [Fig.

The text explains that oersted concluded that moving charges or currents produced a magnetic field in the surrounding space.

The text explains that following this, there was intense experimentation.

The text explains that in 1864, the laws obeyed by electricity and magnetism were unified and formulated by Chapter Four MOVING CHARGES AND MAGNETISM

The text explains that physics 108 James Maxwell who then realised that light was electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that radio waves were discovered by Hertz, and produced by J.C.Bose and G.

The text explains that a remarkable scientific and technological progress took place in the 20th century.

The text explains that this was due to our increased understanding of electromagnetism and the invention of devices for production, amplification, transmission and detection of electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will see how magnetic field exerts forces on moving charged particles, like electrons, protons, and current-carrying wires.

The text explains that 4.2 MAGNETIC FORCE 4.2.1 Sources and fields Before we introduce the concept of a magnetic field B, we shall recapitulate what we have learnt in Chapter 1 about the electric field E.

The text explains that the charge Q, the source of the field, produces an electric field E, where FIGURE 4.1 The magnetic field due to a straight long current-carrying wire.

The text explains that experimentally, it is found to obey the principle of superposition: the magnetic field of several sources is the vector addition of magnetic field of each individual source.

The text explains that the force acts in a (sideways) direction perpendicular to both the velocity and the magnetic field.

The text explains that its direction is given by the screw rule or right hand rule for vector (or cross) product as illustrated in Fig.

The text explains that let the number density of these mobile charge carriers in it be n.

The text explains that now nq vd is the current density j and |(nq vd )|A is the current I (see Chapter 3 for the discussion of current and current density).

The text explains that thus, F = [(nq vd )l A] × B = [ jAl ] ´´´´´ B = Il ´´´´´ B (4.4) where l is a vector of magnitude l, the length of the rod, and with a direction identical to the current I.

The text explains that fIGURE 4.2 The direction of the magnetic force acting on a charged particle.

The text explains that we have learnt in Mechanics (see Class XI book, Chapter 5) that a force on a particle does work if the force has a component along (or opposed to) the direction of motion of the particle.

The text explains that so no work is done and no change in the magnitude of the velocity is produced (though the direction of momentum may be changed). [Notice that this is unlike the force due to an electric field, q E, which can have a component parallel (or antiparallel) to motion and thus can transfer energy in addition to momentum.] We shall consider motion of a charged particle in a uniform magnetic field.

The text explains that its direction* is perpendicular to the plane containing dl and r .

The text explains that the ƒ sign indicates that the field is perpendicular to the plane of this page and directed into it. * The sense of dl × r is also given by the Right Hand Screw rule : Look at the plane containing vectors dl and r.

The text explains that some of these are: (i) Both are long range, since both depend inversely on the square of distance from the source to the point of interest.

The text explains that the principle of superposition applies to both fields. [In this connection, note that the magnetic field is linear in the source I dl just as the electrostatic field is linear in its source: the electric charge.] (ii) The electrostatic field is produced by a scalar source, namely, the electric charge.

The text explains that the magnetic field is produced by a vector source I dl.

The text explains that (iii) The electrostatic field is along the displacement vector joining the source and the field point.

The text explains that 4.7, the magnetic field at any point in the direction of dl (the dashed line) is zero.

The text explains that there is an interesting relation between ε0, the permittivity of free space; μ0, the permeability of free space; and c, the speed of light in vacuum: ( ) 0 0 0 0 4 4 μ ε μ ε = π π ( )7 9 1 10 9 10 − = × 8 2 2 1 1 (3 10 ) c = = × We will discuss this connection further in Chapter 8 on the electromagnetic waves.

The text explains that (4.7)] 2 d 10 m l x − = ∆ = , I = 10 A, r = 0.5 m = y, 7 0 T m / 4 10 A μ − π = θ = 90° ; sin θ = 1 7 2 2 10 10 10 d 25 10 − − − × × = × B = 4 × 10–8 T The direction of the field is in the +z-direction.

The text explains that physics 116 The direction of dB is shown in Fig.

The text explains that the net contribution along x-direction can be obtained by integrating dBx = dB cos θ over the loop.

The text explains that the direction of the magnetic field is given by (another) right-hand thumb rule stated below: Curl the palm of your right hand around the circular wire with the fingers pointing in the direction of the current.

The text explains that the right-hand thumb gives the direction of the magnetic field.

The text explains that the direction of the field is given by the right-hand thumb rule described in the text.

The text explains that hence direction of B for a semicircular arc is given by the right-hand rule and magnitude is half that of a circular loop.

The text explains that (c) Same magnitude of B but opposite in direction to that in (b).

The text explains that then the direction of the thumb gives the sense in which the current I is regarded as positive.

The text explains that (ii) The field direction at any point on this circle is tangential to it.

The text explains that the field is directly proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the distance from the (infinitely long) current source.

The text explains that (iv) There exists a simple rule to determine the direction of the magnetic field due to a long wire.

The text explains that this rule, called the right-hand rule*, is: Grasp the wire in your right hand with your extended thumb pointing in the direction of the current.

The text explains that determine the ratio of (a) current sensitivity and (b) voltage sensitivity of M2 and M1.

The text explains that 4.11 In a chamber, a uniform magnetic field of 6.5 G (1 G = 10–4 T ) is maintained.

The text explains that an electron is shot into the field with a speed of 4.8 × 106 m s–1 normal to the field.

The text explains that explain why the path of the electron is a circle.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4: Moving Charges and Magnetism

prelims_traps: Do not treat 4.1 INTRODUCTION and MOVING CHARGES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Moving Charges and Magnetism; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Moving Charges and Magnetism through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 4.1 INTRODUCTION and MOVING CHARGES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
